questions.q: What is the primary advantage of a monolithic architecture?
options: It scales infinitely without any performance degradation
It's simpler to develop, test, and deploy as a single unit initially
It automatically handles distributed system failures
It requires no database and stores everything in memory
questions.explanation: Monoliths are straightforward: one codebase, one process, easier initial development and deployment. As systems grow, they become harder to scale and maintain. Infinite scaling, automatic failure handling, and eliminating databases are not monolith advantages.

questions.q: What is a key challenge when transitioning from a monolith to microservices?
options: Microservices cannot access the same database
Managing distributed transactions and ensuring consistency across independent services becomes complex
Microservices don't support authentication
All microservices must run on the same hardware
questions.explanation: Transitioning to microservices introduces distributed system complexity: transactions span multiple services, data consistency is harder to achieve, and operational complexity increases. Databases can be shared or separate (per design). Auth, hardware constraints aren't inherent challenges.

questions.q: What is load balancing in the context of server architecture?
options: Removing unneeded features to reduce application size
Distributing incoming requests across multiple servers to prevent overload and improve availability
Balancing the CPU time between different threads
Equalizing database query costs across tables
questions.explanation: Load balancing distributes requests across multiple servers to prevent bottlenecks, improve throughput, and provide fault tolerance. Feature removal, thread scheduling, and query optimization are separate concerns.

questions.q: What does the principle of statelessness mean in server architecture?
options: Servers don't use databases
Servers don't store session information; all request context is provided by the client or centralized session store
Servers can only handle one client at a time
Servers cannot log information about requests
questions.explanation: Stateless servers don't retain client-specific information between requests. All state either comes from the client or an external store, enabling easy horizontal scaling and fault tolerance. Databases, concurrency, and logging are unrelated.

options: A single server's hardware (CPU, RAM) is upgraded
Additional identical servers are added to handle more load
The application source code is optimized for better performance
The database is sharded into smaller parts
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more servers to distribute load (scaling out). Vertical scaling upgrades a single server's hardware (scaling up). Code optimization and database sharding are separate techniques.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a reverse proxy in server architecture?
options: To hide the client's IP address from the server
To forward client requests to backend servers, handle load balancing, SSL termination, and caching
To encrypt all traffic between client and server
To store user passwords securely
questions.explanation: A reverse proxy sits between clients and backend servers, forwarding requests, distributing load, terminating SSL/TLS, and caching responses. It doesn't hide clients, encrypt (SSL does), or store passwords. A forward proxy hides client identity.

questions.q: What is a circuit breaker in microservices architecture?
options: A physical component that disconnects overloaded servers
A pattern that stops sending requests to a failing service, allowing it to recover before resuming
A security feature that blocks malicious IP addresses
A database connection pooling mechanism
questions.explanation: A circuit breaker (fail-fast pattern) monitors service health and stops forwarding requests to failing services, allowing them to recover. When health improves, it gradually resumes traffic. It's not physical hardware, security, or connection pooling.

questions.q: What does eventual consistency mean in distributed systems?
options: All servers must be in sync before acknowledging writes
Data is replicated eventually, and all nodes will converge to the same state after a period of no updates, but temporary inconsistencies may exist
Consistency is never guaranteed and conflicts are ignored
Only the primary database maintains the correct state
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency allows temporary divergence across distributed replicas but guarantees convergence when updates cease. It trades immediate consistency for higher availability and lower latency. Strong consistency requires synchronous replication; ignoring conflicts creates problems; primary-only isn't distributed.

questions.q: In containerized server architecture, what does orchestration (like Kubernetes) provide?
options: Automatically writing application code based on deployment needs
Managing container deployment, scaling, networking, storage, and recovery from failures across a cluster
Compressing container images to reduce size
Encrypting communication between containers
questions.explanation: Orchestrators automate container lifecycle management: deployment, scaling, networking, service discovery, and self-healing. They don't write code, compress images (that's image optimization), or handle encryption (separate concern).

questions.q: What is the difference between a service mesh and traditional load balancing?
options: Service meshes are only for monolithic applications
A service mesh provides fine-grained control over inter-service communication, security policies, and observability at the network level without modifying application code
Load balancers and service meshes are synonymous terms
Service meshes can only be used with containers
questions.explanation: Service meshes (like Istio) manage service-to-service communication, security, retries, and observability transparently. Load balancers distribute traffic but lack service mesh features. Service meshes work with containers and non-containers; they're independent of app structure (monolith or microservices).
